title: Rigid heddle linen sampler
subtitle: Weaving a small swatch to explore linen's character before a larger project
excerpt: Linen warp behaves differently from cotton: it has less elasticity, resists take-up, and produces a crisper, harder hand. This sampler covers warping, weaving, and wet-finishing a small linen swatch to understand how the yarn handles before committing to a larger project.
sourceNotes: Linen weaving sampler practice synthesised from standard rigid-heddle handweaving guides. Linen's behaviour is described in The Handweaver's Pattern Directory (Ann Sutton, 2003, in copyright, substance only referenced).

glossaryTerms.definition: The lengthwise threads stretched on the loom. Linen warp has very little elasticity, which affects how it threads through the heddle and how the edges behave during weaving.
Washing and pressing a woven piece after it comes off the loom to full the fibres, remove weaving tension, and set the final hand. Linen softens noticeably with wet-finishing.

The number of warp threads per inch or centimetre. Linen at 10/2 weight suits a 10 dpi reed; a sett that is too open will produce a weave that pulls apart, while a sett too tight will be stiff and hard to beat.
Plain weave. Linen tabby produces a smooth, strong fabric ideal for table and kitchen textiles.

content.text: Linen behaves quite differently from cotton or wool on the rigid heddle loom. It does not stretch or give during threading, it resists the heddle more than cotton, and it finishes to a much crisper hand. A 20 cm sampler is a practical way to encounter these differences before warping a full table-linen project. The
results observed on the sampler guide all the subsequent decisions for the main piece.

items.name: 10/2 linen warp yarn, natural or bleached

content.text: for a warp of about 20 cm width. Linen does not stretch to accommodate a sloppy thread path; pull each thread clearly through its slot or hole with the reed hook and ensure no thread crosses another in the heddle. Linen threads break if forced; be deliberate and even-handed.
Weave a 30 cm length in plain

. Beat firmly; linen benefits from a slightly firmer beat than cotton to produce a dense, smooth surface. Linen also has a tendency to stick in the heddle slots as you advance the warp; give a gentle press on the warp beam to re-tension before each beat. The selvedge edges will be less elastic than cotton and may feel tighter; this is normal.

Cut off the sampler with 5 cm fringe at each end. Hand-wash in warm water with a small amount of gentle detergent. Rinse thoroughly. Press firmly with a hot iron while still damp. Linen gains clarity, smoothness, and softness through this press; a linen sampler that feels harsh off the loom is usually quite pleasant after ironing. Record the measurements before and after washing to calculate the actual shrinkage rate for your yarn and sett combination.

items.problem: The linen warp breaks repeatedly during threading
items.cause: The thread path through the heddle is too tight or the reed hook is snagging the yarn
items.fix: Work slowly and pull directly along the slot or hole path rather than at an angle. Linen has minimal stretch; any lateral pull stresses the yarn at the heddle edge.

items.problem: The finished sampler feels very stiff and rough
items.cause: It has not been wet-finished, or a too-fine yarn at too tight a sett was used
items.fix: Always wet-finish linen before judging the hand. A hot wash and firm hot-iron press will soften the fabric considerably. If it remains rough after finishing, the sett may be too tight for the yarn weight.
